rules.reason: VCs almost universally require Delaware C-Corp. Preferred stock, drag-along rights, and standard VC mechanics require C-Corp structure.

rules.signal: Solo founder, bootstrapped, no plans to raise
rules.reason: LLC provides liability protection with pass-through taxation and minimal formality for solo founders not seeking VC.

rules.signal: Mission-driven, wants to signal social purpose
rules.reason: Delaware Public Benefit Corporation (PBC) provides legal protection for mission-driven decisions and is compatible with VC investment.

rules.signal: Non-US citizen or non-permanent resident co-founder
rules.reason: S-Corps cannot have non-US citizen/non-permanent-resident shareholders (IRC Section 1361). C-Corp is required.

rules.signal: Wants pass-through taxation, all US citizen owners
rules.reason: LLC provides pass-through taxation with flexibility. S-Corp also qualifies but has strict eligibility requirements.
S-Corps are limited to 100 shareholders (IRC Section 1361). C-Corp has no shareholder limit.

rules.signal: Home state operations only, no VC plans
rules.reason: Registering in home state avoids the cost and complexity of foreign qualification. Home state LLC is simpler and cheaper for purely local operations.

rules.signal: Wants privacy and low annual fees
rules.reason: Wyoming offers strong LLC privacy protections, no state income tax, and low annual fees. Good alternative for bootstrapped founders who don't need Delaware prestige.

rules.signal: Has non-US citizen or non-permanent resident co-founder
rules.reason: S-Corps cannot have non-US shareholders (IRC Section 1361). C-Corp is the only VC-compatible option for teams with international founders.

rules.signal: Plans to issue stock options to employees or contractors
rules.reason: Only C-Corps can issue ISOs (Incentive Stock Options) with favorable tax treatment. LLCs use complex profit interest units instead. A 409A valuation is required before any option grants.

rules.signal: Plans to seek investment from institutional VCs
rules.reason: Institutional VCs almost universally require Delaware C-Corp structure. VC funds structured as partnerships typically cannot hold LLC interests due to UBTI issues.

disqualifiers.condition: Non-US citizen or non-permanent resident shareholder
disqualifiers.reason: IRC Section 1361(b)(1)(C) prohibits S-Corps from having non-resident alien shareholders.
S-Corps are limited to 100 shareholders under IRC Section 1361.
S-Corps can only have one class of stock. Preferred stock (required by VCs) disqualifies S-Corp status.

disqualifiers.condition: Corporate shareholder (another corporation or LLC as investor)
disqualifiers.reason: S-Corps cannot be owned by corporations or partnerships — only individuals, certain trusts, and estates.

disqualifiers.condition: Operating in California as a non-licensed-professional business
disqualifiers.reason: California LLPs are only available to licensed professionals (attorneys, CPAs, architects). General startups cannot form LLPs in California.

disqualifiers.condition: S-Corp election (Form 2553) not filed within 75 days of incorporation
disqualifiers.reason: The entity is treated as a C-Corp for that entire tax year. Pass-through status is lost permanently for that year.

disqualifiers.condition: Operating in California without also filing CA Form 3560
disqualifiers.reason: California treats the entity as a C-Corp without a separate state S-Corp election. The federal Form 2553 is not sufficient.
disqualifiers.source: CA Revenue and Taxation Code Section 23800.5

disqualifiers.condition: Any business with meaningful liability exposure, co-founders, employees, or customers
disqualifiers.reason: All partners bear UNLIMITED personal liability for ALL debts and obligations, including actions of other partners. Convert to LLC or Corp immediately.

all_entities.consequence_if_missed: Cannot open bank account or hire employees
$500/day civil penalty; up to $10,000 + prison for willful violation

c_corp_and_s_corp.consequence_if_missed: $250 late fee + risk of administrative suspension

c_corp_and_s_corp.deadline: Within 30 days of stock grant — NO EXCEPTIONS
c_corp_and_s_corp.consequence_if_missed: Ordinary income tax on full vested value as it vests — potentially hundreds of thousands of dollars
Company may not own the IP it was built on

s_corp_only.deadline: Within 75 days of incorporation — ALL shareholders must sign
s_corp_only.consequence_if_missed: Treated as C-Corp; pass-through status permanently lost for that year
California treats entity as C-Corp without this separate state election

c_corp_only.deadline: Before first option grant; refresh at each round or annually
c_corp_only.consequence_if_missed: Option holders face immediate ordinary income tax + 20% excise tax under IRC Section 409A
